raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 1.0,
    "R1.3": 1.0,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.0,
    "R1.6": 0.3,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The paragraph's central thesis is that high curvature implies tighter greedy guarantees, motivating CurvMax; the validity of this conceptual claim is foundational and must be reconstructed carefully.",
    "R1.2": "The curvature-aware bound (1/κ)(1−e^(−κ)) is monotonically decreasing in κ on [0,1], so higher curvature produces a weaker, not tighter, guarantee — the paragraph has the direction of the inequality reversed, a load-bearing logical error that propagates through the entire argument.",
    "R1.3": "The empirical results (baseline 0.83, CurvMax 0.66) show performance degrading under higher curvature, which actually corroborates the correct reading of the bound but directly contradicts the authors' stated interpretation; the dismissal of this discrepancy as finite-sample noise must be scrutinised.",
    "R1.7": "Two specific attributions are invoked (Nemhauser–Wolsey–Fisher 1978 for the (1−1/e) guarantee; Conforti–Cornuéjols 1984 for the curvature refinement); verifying that the curvature result is correctly attributed and not conflated with later formulations (e.g., Sviridenko et al.) is material to scholarly credibility."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Is your primary concern (a) evaluating the technical and logical soundness of the mathematical claims and experimental interpretation, or (b) reviewing and improving the prose quality and writing style?"
}

rationale.R1.1: The paragraph's central thesis is that high curvature implies tighter greedy guarantees, motivating CurvMax; the validity of this conceptual claim is foundational and must be reconstructed carefully.
rationale.R1.2: The curvature-aware bound (1/κ)(1−e^(−κ)) is monotonically decreasing in κ on [0,1], so higher curvature produces a weaker, not tighter, guarantee — the paragraph has the direction of the inequality reversed, a load-bearing logical error that propagates through the entire argument.
rationale.R1.3: The empirical results (baseline 0.83, CurvMax 0.66) show performance degrading under higher curvature, which actually corroborates the correct reading of the bound but directly contradicts the authors' stated interpretation; the dismissal of this discrepancy as finite-sample noise must be scrutinised.
rationale.R1.7: Two specific attributions are invoked (Nemhauser–Wolsey–Fisher 1978 for the (1−1/e) guarantee; Conforti–Cornuéjols 1984 for the curvature refinement); verifying that the curvature result is correctly attributed and not conflated with later formulations (e.g., Sviridenko et al.) is material to scholarly credibility.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary concern (a) evaluating the technical and logical soundness of the mathematical claims and experimental interpretation, or (b) reviewing and improving the prose quality and writing style?
